subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
title: Classification of Industries based on Pollution Index (PI)
focus: Criteria, categories, and background of industrial classification by CPCB/MoEFCC.
rationale: Highly examinable for Prelims, particularly matching categories with PI scores and examples, and understanding the specific exemptions for White and Blue categories.
sectionExcerpt: Revised methodology followed based on Pollution Index (PI) - ranges from 0 to 100 with increasing value indicating increased degree of pollution load. PI is determined based on Precautionary Principle... formulated in 2016... Categories: Red (PI > 80): No Red category industries shall normally be permitted in ecologically fragile area. Orange (55 <= PI < 80). Green (25 <= PI < 55). White (PI < 25): Non-polluting; do not require Environmental Clearance (EC) and Consent. Blue: Includes Essential Environmental Services (ESSs) for management of waste... Originated in 1989 with Doon Valley (Uttarakhand) Notification.
evidenceBullets: Pollution Index (PI) ranges from 0 to 100, formulated in 2016 based on the Precautionary Principle evaluating water, air, and hazardous waste.
Red Category (PI > 80) industries are normally not permitted in ecologically fragile or protected areas.
White Category (PI < 25) industries are non-polluting and do not require Environmental Clearance (EC) or Consent.
Blue Category includes Essential Environmental Services (ESSs) like Municipal Solid Waste Management and sewage treatment plants.
The classification of industries originated in 1989 with the Doon Valley Notification issued by MoEFCC.

subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
title: Environment Protection (Management of Contaminated Sites) Rules, 2025
focus: Key provisions, exclusions, and liabilities under the new Contaminated Sites Rules.
rationale: New environmental rules are prime targets for UPSC, especially the specific exclusions (radioactive, mining, oil spills) and the application of the polluter pays principle.
sectionExcerpt: MoEFCC notified Environment Protection (Management of Contaminated Sites) Rules, 2025. Contaminants Covered: Hazardous substances under Hazardous and Other Wastes Rules, 2016. Exclusions: Contamination from radioactive waste, mining, oil spills at sea, and solid waste dumps... Polluter Identification: SPCBs identify the polluter. If land is sold, the new owner is liable. Clean-Up Plan: Polluter must carry out a clean-up plan... Initial assessment costs may be covered by Central Government from Environmental Relief Fund under Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991.
evidenceBullets: Notified by MoEFCC to manage sites polluted by hazardous waste disposal.
Explicitly excludes contamination from radioactive waste, mining, oil spills at sea, and solid waste dumps.
SPCBs identify the polluter; if the contaminated land is sold, the new owner assumes liability.
The polluter must execute and fund a clean-up plan through an approved agency.
Initial assessment costs may be covered by the Environmental Relief Fund under the Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991.

subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
title: Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Amendment Rules, 2025
focus: Introduction of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) for non-ferrous metals and recycling targets.
rationale: EPR frameworks are frequently tested. The specific inclusion of non-ferrous metals, the progressive targets, and the certificate validity are key testable facts.
sectionExcerpt: Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Amendment Rules, 2025 were notified by MoEFCC... Introduces comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for Scrap of Non-Ferrous Metals (including aluminium, copper and zinc and its alloys)... Targets: Every producer shall be responsible for recycling target structure, which progressively increases from 10% in 2026-2027 to 75% by 2032-2033. Central Pollution Control Board: Shall generate an EPR certificate... Validity of the EPR certificate: Two years from the end of financial year when generated.
evidenceBullets: Introduces an Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework specifically for Scrap of Non-Ferrous Metals like aluminium, copper, and zinc.
Recycling targets progressively increase from 10% in 2026-2027 to 75% by 2032-2033.
The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) is responsible for generating EPR certificates through a portal.
EPR certificates are valid for two years from the end of the financial year in which they are generated.

subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
title: WHO Global Conference on Air Pollution and Health
focus: Objectives and India's commitments at the WHO Global Conference.
rationale: International conferences and India's specific targets (like NCAP) are standard Prelims material.
sectionExcerpt: WHO Global Conference on Air Pollution and Health: Co-organized by WHO and Columbia and other UN Agencies like UNEP, WMO etc. Objectives: Accelerating action for clean air, clean energy access and climate change mitigation. Key Highlights: Over 50 countries, committed to shared goal of reducing health impacts of air pollution by 50% by 2040. India reaffirmed its commitment by 2040 under National Clean Air Programme.
evidenceBullets: Co-organized by WHO, Columbia, UNEP, and WMO to accelerate action for clean air and climate change mitigation.
Over 50 countries committed to reducing the health impacts of air pollution by 50% by 2040.
India reaffirmed its 2040 commitment under the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP).

subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
title: Smog Eating Technology
focus: Mechanism and application of photocatalytic coatings for pollution reduction.
rationale: Science and Technology applied to Environment is a high-yield UPSC topic. The use of TiO2 and UV light is a highly testable mechanism.
sectionExcerpt: Smog Eating Technology: Delhi Government ordered study on use of Smog Eating photocatalytic coatings on roads. Objective: Reduce nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and harmful hydrocarbons in public spaces... Mechanism - Photocatalytic activity: Uses light energy to break down harmful pollutants & organic waste. Usually uses titanium dioxide (TiO2) coatings as photocatalysis under UV light to decompose NOx.
evidenceBullets: Delhi Government is studying Smog Eating photocatalytic coatings on roads to reduce NO2 and hydrocarbons.
The technology uses light energy (photocatalytic activity) to break down harmful pollutants and organic waste.
It typically utilizes titanium dioxide (TiO2) coatings under UV light to decompose nitrogen oxides (NOx).

subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
title: Global Outlook Council on Water
focus: Launch and association of the Global Outlook Council on Water.
rationale: Short factual snippet about a new international initiative linked to the G20, suitable for a statement in a multi-statement question.
sectionExcerpt: Global Outlook Council on Water: The President of South Africa announced its launch as a G20 Presidential Legacy Initiative.
evidenceBullets: Launched by the President of South Africa.
Established as a G20 Presidential Legacy Initiative.
